coredata.dc:title: Chiral recognition and quantitative analysis of tyrosine enantiomers using L-cysteine capped CdTe quantum dots: Circular dichroism, fluorescence, and theoretical calculation studies
coredata.dc:creator: Feizi, Foroozan
Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Barati, Ali
Gholivand, Mohammad Bagher
Mousavi, Farimah
coredata.dc:description: In this work, L-cysteine-capped CdTe quantum dots (L-Cys/CdTe QDs) were used as novel chiral probes for in enantioselective recognition and quantitative determination of tyrosine (Tyr) enantiomers using both fluorescence and circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopies. Different quenching percentages in fluorescence of L-Cys/CdTe QDs in the presence of similar concentrations of Tyr enantiomers were clearly observed. Quantum mechanical (QM) calculations were also used to confirm such different interactions of L- and D-Tyr enantiomers with L-Cys/CdTe QDs. However, the CD spectroscopy exhibited higher capability and sensitivity, compared to fluorescence spectroscopy, to distinguish between these enantiomers. While, Tyr enantiomers provided no sensible CD signals at micromolar concentration levels, they exhibited remarkable and opposite CD spectra at these concentration levels in the presence of L-Cys/CdTe QDs. Under the optimized conditions, the CD signals of L- and D-Tyr enantiomers showed good linear relationships with the concentrations of both enantiomers over the range of 10–80 µM with limit of detections (LODs) of 1.5 and 1.6 µM, respectively. This method was also successfully applied to enantiomeric excess determination of Tyr at the total concentration of 50 μM with a LOD of 1.2%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Enantiomeric excess
Chiral recognition
L-Cys/CdTe quantum dots
Circular dichroism
Fluorescence

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial support of this work by Iran Science Elites Federation and Research Council of Razi University is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption (red) and emission (green) spectra of the synthesized L-Cys/CdTe QDs. Inset shows the TEM image of QDs.

Fluorescence spectra of L-Cys capped CdTe QDs in the presence of 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 120, 150, and 200 μM of L-Tyr (a) and D-Tyr (b) at λex = 340 nm. The corresponding Stern-Volmer calibration curves of (a) and (b) in the concentration range of 5–90 μM (at λem = 530 nm). (d) The fluorescence signals of the probe toward Tyr enantiomers and L-enantiomers of all amino acids at 100 μM.

The geometrically optimized structures of (A) CdTe, (B) L-Cys/CdTe, (C) L-Tyr-L-Cys/CdTe, (D) D-Tyr-L-Cys/CdTe, (E) D-Cys/CdTe, (F) L-Tyr-D-Cys/CdTe, and (G) D-Tyr-D-Cys/CdTe with their corresponding molecular orbital energy diagrams.

(a) CD spectra of 100 µM of Tyr enantiomers, 5.0 × 10-5 M L-Cys/CdTe QDs, and 10 µM of Tyr enantiomers in the presence of 5.0 × 10-5 M L-Cys/CdTe QDs. b) CD spectra of 50 µM L-Tyr in the presence of 1.0 × 10-5-1.0 × 10-4 M L-Cys/CdTe QDs. Inset shows the corresponding CDs intensities at 272 nm (the concentration of QDs was calculated using the original concentration of cadmium source).

(a) CD spectra of L-Tyr and D-Tyr from 0 to 100 μM in the presence of L-Cys/ CdTe QDs (5.0 × 10-5 M). (b) The corresponding calibration curve between the CD signals at 232 and 272 nm and concentrations of L- (red points) and D-Tyr (blue points) enantiomers.
CD spectra of 50 μM of L-amino acid in the presence of L-Cys/CdTe QDs (5.0 × 10-5 M).

(a) CD spectra of 50 μM of Tyr with different enantiomeric excess values (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100%) in the presence of L-Cys/CdTe QDs (5.0 × 10-5 M). (b) and (c) The corresponding calibration curves at wavelengths 272 nm (b) and 232 nm (c). Negative enantiomeric excess values correspond to the excess of L-Tyr and positive values correspond to the excess of D-Tyr.
The computational results driven from quantum mechanical study distinguishing L- and D-Tyr by L- and D-Cys-capped CdTe QD.

head.ce:title: Chiral recognition and quantitative analysis of tyrosine enantiomers using L-cysteine capped CdTe quantum dots: Circular dichroism, fluorescence, and theoretical calculation studies

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, L-cysteine-capped CdTe quantum dots (L-Cys/CdTe QDs) were used as novel chiral probes for in enantioselective recognition and quantitative determination of tyrosine (Tyr) enantiomers using both fluorescence and circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopies. Different quenching percentages in fluorescence of L-Cys/CdTe QDs in the presence of similar concentrations of Tyr enantiomers were clearly observed. Quantum mechanical (QM) calculations were also used to confirm such different interactions of L- and D-Tyr enantiomers with L-Cys/CdTe QDs. However, the CD spectroscopy exhibited higher capability and sensitivity, compared to fluorescence spectroscopy, to distinguish between these enantiomers. While, Tyr enantiomers provided no sensible CD signals at micromolar concentration levels, they exhibited remarkable and opposite CD spectra at these concentration levels in the presence of L-Cys/CdTe QDs. Under the optimized conditions, the CD signals of L- and D-Tyr enantiomers showed good linear relationships with the concentrations of both enantiomers over the range of 10–80 µM with limit of detections (LODs) of 1.5 and 1.6 µM, respectively. This method was also successfully applied to enantiomeric excess determination of Tyr at the total concentration of 50 μM with a LOD of 1.2%.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to different functionality of enantiomers of the most chiral species, their identification and enantiomeric excess determination is of highly importance especially for clinical applications [1]. Until now, many different methods such as chiral electrophoresis methods [2], chiral chromatography methods [3], enzymatic methods [4], and spectroscopic and fluorimetric methods based on a number of different chiral probes [5,6] have been employed for enantiomeric excess determination, chiral identification, and enantio-separation of various chiral species.

In recent years, the use of fluorescent chiral nanoparticles as chiroptical probes for enantiomeric excess determination and chiral identification has attracted an increasing research interest [7–10]. The chiral optical activity of these nanoparticles is commonly originated from the intrinsic chiral structure of the nanoparticles [11–14], the chiral properties of their capping chiral ligands [15] or from the chiral memory effects [16]. The chiral semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [17–19] are among the widely reported chiral nanoparticles that have been applied for various purposes from enantiomeric sensing to enantiomeric separation [20,21], and cell imaging [22,23].

The first chiral QDs were reported by Yurii K. Gun’ko et al. who capped the surface of CdS QDs by D- or L-penicillamine ligands. The circular dichroism studies of these QDs produced intense mirror CD spectra and those capped with a racemic mixture of penicillamine ligands showed only a very weak signal [24]. This simple method has been extensively used to preparation of chiral QDs with the aim of chiral recognition and enantiomeric excess determination of chiral components. For example, the fluorescence signal of chiral L-cysteine (Cys)-capped CdSe(ZnS) QDs exhibited selective responses to carnitine enantiomers in aqueous solution. The fluorescence intensities of L-Cys-capped QDs and D-Cys-capped QDs were only quenched in the presence of D- and L-carnitine enantiomers, respectively, and were not affected by the other enantiomer [25]. Cyclodextrins have also been used as chiral agents for capping the surface of QDs [20,26]. In this regard, Haibing Li and co-workers modified the surface of CdSe/ZnS QDs with cyclodextrin through a simple sonochemical method to prepare an enantioselective fluorescent sensor for amino acid enantiomers [26]. Interestingly, when cyclodextrin‐QDs treated with L‐amino acids, they showed a much greater fluorescence enhancement, compared with those treated with D‐amino acids.

Amino acids are chiral compounds that have been used not only as chiral building blocks in organic synthesis, but also as chiral auxiliaries and source of stereo-genic centers [27]. Moreover, recent researches have demonstrated that amino acids can be considered as biomarkers for detection of cancer [28–32]. The importance of chiral recognition of amino acids is that the backbone of peptides and proteins are commonly formed by the L- and the D-amino acids are usually found in trace levels. Therefore, the amount of D-amino acids can be employed for clinical diagnosis of some diseases. In fact, a potential relationship between the amounts of D-amino acids and kidney function has been indicated in some reports [33].

In this work we used fluorescence and circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopic techniques for chiral identification and enantiomer excess determination of L- and D-tyrosine (Tyr) enantiomers by using L-Cys/CdTe QDs as chiroptical probes.

Amino acid enantiomers, CdCl2·2.5 H2O, NaBH4 and tellurium powder were purchased from Sigma Chemical Company. All other chemicals were provided from Merck. The CD spectra were recorded on a JASCO spectropolarimeter Model J-810 with a scanning rate of 200 nm min−1 and fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer.

CdTe QDs were prepared in aqueous solution using the method described in our previously reported paper [34]. Briefly, NaHTe was prepared as follows: First, 0.05 mmol Te powder and 1 mmol NaBH4 were added to a flask containing 5 mL water and was stirred under N2 until all Te powder reduced and the solution becomes colorless. In the second step, 0.1 mmol CdCl2·2.5 H2O and 3 mmol L-Cys were dissolved in 50 mL deionized water. The mixture was stirred for 30 min and the pH of resulting solution was adjusted to 10.0 using NaOH solution. Finally, the solution of NaHTe obtained from the first step was quickly injected into the Cd2+ and L-Cys solution under N2 and stirring for 30 min. The obtained orange solution was placed into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in oven at 100 °C for 2. After this time, the resulting L-Cys/CdTe QDs solution was allowed to cool down to room temperature.

The fluorescence spectroscopy was used to investigate the fluorescence response of L-Cys/CdTe QDs toward Tyr enantiomers. Firstly, the stock solutions of 1 mM D- and L-Tyr in one percent hydrochloric acid were prepared. The emission spectra were collected by titration of L-Cys/CdTe QD (5.0 × 10-5 M, as calculated using the original cadmium source) with 10–100 μM of D- and L-Tyr enantiomers (diluted from their stock solutions) with an excitation wavelength set at 360 nm, where Tyr enantiomers cannot be excited.

The CD spectra of Tyr enantiomers in the concentration range of 10–100 μM and their mixture with different enantiomeric excess values in the presence of L-Cys/CdTe QDs were recorded over 200 to 300 nm region after 10 min incubation at 25 °C.

The computational models have drawn and geometrically optimized by Merck molecular force field (MMFF94) [35,36] with further semi-empirical single point calculations via applying the parameterized hybrid of ab-initio (restricted Hartree-Fock) and semi-empirical quantum mechanical method (RHF/PM6) [37], using the Spartan ‘16 package to confirm the binding mode and interaction energy between Tyr enantiomers and L-Cys/CdTe QDs.

Characterization of the synthesized L-Cys/CdTe QDs was carried out using UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopies and TEM images. As can be seen from Fig. 1 , the QDs exhibit an excitation-independent, narrow and symmetric fluorescence emission spectrum with a maximum intensity at 530 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized QDs was obtained as 0.3, relative to fluorescein as a reference dye with QY of 0.93 at 490 nm in a sodium borate buffer solution of pH 9.5. The TEM image of QDs (inset of Fig. 1) specify roughly spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter of 2.9 nm. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of QDs exhibited a broad peak with the first excitonic absorption peak at about 530 nm, which indicated the average size of the QDs as about 3.0 nm, in accordance to the reported value [38]. To verify the existence of L-Cys on the surface of the prepared QDs, we compared the FTIR spectra of free L-Cys and L-Cys capped CdTe QDs (Fig. S1). The FTIR spectrum of pure L-Cys showed a characteristic peak at 2548 cm−1 assigned to the stretching vibrations of S–H groups that is disappeared in the FTIR spectrum of L-Cys capped CdTe QDs that reveals the covalent attaching of the thiol group of L-Cys to Cd2+ atoms on the surface of QDs.

While there was no meaningful difference between the absorption spectra of L-Cys/CdTe QDs in the presence of D- and L-Tyr (Fig. S2), the amount of fluorescence quenching of QDs in the presence of the same concentration of these enantiomers was different. Fig. 2 a and 2b show the recorded emission spectra of L-Cys/CdTe QDs in the course of titration with 5–200 μM of L- and D-Tyr enantiomers, respectively. As illustrated, the emission spectrum of QDs quench without any wavelength shift, upon the addition of Tyr enantiomers. However, the quenching effect of the L-Tyr was found to be higher than that of the D-Tyr so that the fluorescence of QDs quenched by about 37% and 28% in the presence of 100 μM of these enantiomers, respectively.

The relationship between the QDs fluorescence intensity and concentration of L- and D-Tyr enantiomers are shown in Fig. 2c, both of which following the Stern-Volmer equation of F0/F = 1 + Ksv[Q], where, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively, [Q] is the molar concentration of the quencher, and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. The Stern-Volmer plots revealed satisfactory linear relationships between F0/F versus concentrations of L- and D-Tyr enantiomers over the range of 5–90 µM with the linear correlation coefficients 0.9932 and 0.9944 and Ksv values of 0.0052 and 0.0031, respectively. The limit of detections (LODs) of the method for L- and D-Tyr enantiomers, evaluated based on equation LOD = 3S0/m (n = 4), where S0 is the standard deviation of blank measurements and m is the slope of linear calibration curve, were 2.9 and 4.8 µM, respectively,

Selectivity of the designed L-Cys/CdTe QDs fluorescence sensor for the L- and D-Tyr enantiomers over other amino acids was studied by comparison of their signals with those obtained for the L-enantiomers of a large number other amino acids including alanine, arginine, asparagine, aspartic acid, glutamine, glutamic acid, glycine, histidine, isoleucine, leucine, lysine, methionine, phenylanine, proline, serine, threonine, tryptophane, and valine, all at 100 μM concentration, and the results are shown in Fig. 2d. As seen, the probe shows very weak responses to other amino acids, compared to those for the Tyr enantiomers.

To explain the different quenching effects of L- and D-Tyr enantiomers on the fluorescence of L-Cys/CdTe QD, the molecular orbital energies of QDs in the presence of Tyr enantiomers were investigated by using quantum mechanical calculations. To this purpose, a typical tetrahedral CdTe model with ten Cd and twenty Te atoms with a diameter of 13.557 Å was used as the QD basis (Fig. 3 A). This study showed that Cys enantiomers have a different interaction with the CdTe cores, as the bandgap of L- and D-Cys/CdTe QDs has the small difference of 0.11 eV that can be a result of different thermodynamics due to differences inpreferential arrangements of the atoms in space (Fig. 3B and E). The Cys molecules adsorbed on QDs surfaces not only preserve their own chirality but also induce chirality on the CdTe core. In the case of L-Cys/CdTe QDs, a L-Cys molecule was bonded to the CdTe tetrahedral structure as a chiral ligand by exchanging the dangling of Te atom in the apex of CdTe QD model with the S atom of the L-Cys molecule, regarding the strong interaction between Cd and S atoms (Fig. 3B). A variation in the binding energy of L-Cys/CdTe QDs from −16664.63 kJ mol−1, before bonding, to −17277.10 kJ mol−1, after bonding of the L-Cys molecule, well documented the stabilization of QD through the surface bonding with this chiral molecule.

In the next step, the separate attachments of each L- and D-Tyr molecules to the simulated L-Cys/CdTe QDs and D-Cys/CdTe ODs were investigated and the results are shown in Fig. 3. Interestingly, Tyr enantiomers found to have different interactions with the bothe L-Cys/ or D-Cys/CdTe QDs (Table 1 ). Furthermore, the molecular orbitals of these structures presented distinct values for the energies of the highest occupied molecular orbital (EHOMO), the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (ELUMO), and the bandgap (ΔEHOMO-LUMO). As is obvious, the HOMO-LUMO gap of the L- and D-Tyr enantiomers attached to the L-Cys/CdTe QDs showed a difference of 0.49 eV. Moreover, the interaction of the L-Cys/CdTe QDs with L-Tyr molecule resulted in a structure with a HOMO energy of 0.25 eV lower than its interaction with the D-Tyr enantiomer. In summary, these results have presented that the interaction between L-Tyr with L-Cys/CdTe as well as D-Tyr with D-Cys/CdTe is more favorable which have observed by lower EHOMO, higher bandgap, and lower binding energy values. The lower values of EHOMO and the binding energy of L-Tyr-L-Cys/CdTe QDs determines a stronger interaction of this QD with L-Tyr enantiomer compared to D-Tyr enantiomer, which clarified the effective role of the prepared L-Cys/CdTe QDs as an enantioselective probe.

Generally, the chiral enantiomers can be distinguished by polarized lights. The common spectroscopic method used to study chiroptical properties of enantiomers is circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy, in which the difference in the absorption of the left and right circularly lights is measured. Despite the fact that the Tyr enantiomers are chiral molecules and show two visual mirror peaks at about 205 and 230 nm and a very weak peak at 260–280 nm in their CD spectra, when their concentrations are as high as 1 mM (Fig. S3), no meaningful CD signal for this amino acid is observed at lower concentrations up to 100 μM (Figs. S3 and 4a). Therefore, CD spectroscopy cannot be used for direct detection and identification of Tyr enantiomers at low concentrations.

On the other hand, while L-Cys/CdTe QDs are expected to be optically active they exhibited no sensible CD peaks at the concentration used in the course of measurements (Fig. 4 a). However, a 10 μM of Tyr enantiomers in the presence of L-Cys/CdTe QDs provided two intense positive CD peaks at around 232 and 272 nm for L-Tyr and two mirror negative peaks for D-Tyr. These CD signals can be attributed to the interaction of Tyr enantiomers with the L-Cys/CdTe QDs that amplify the CD signal of the Tyr enantiomers. Since the amount of QDs significantly affected the intensity of the CD peaks, the QDs concentration was firstly optimized by varying the amount of QDs from 1.0 × 10-5-1.0 × 10-4 M in the presence of 50 μM of L-Tyr. From the results presented in Fig. 4b, the optimum concentration of QDs that provided the highest CD signal was found to be 5.0 × 10-5 M.

The CD spectra of L-Cys/CdTe QDs in the presence of different concentrations of Tyr enantiomers from 10 to 100 µM, under the optimized conditions, were recorded and are shown in Fig. 5 a. As seen, the intensity of both CD peaks at 232 and 272 nm regularly increased, without any significant shift, by increasing the concentration of Tyr enantiomers. This method provided a good linear relationship between the CD signal and the concentration of Tyr enantiomers in the concentration range of 10–80 μM for both CD peaks (Fig. 5b). It should be noted that both Tyr enantiomers at the same concentration range, but in the absence of CdTe QDs, presented no detectable CD spectra. The LODs of the proposed sensing method based on the three standard deviation of CD signals of the L-Cys/CdTe QDs for L- and D-Tyr at 232 nm that presented a higher sensitivity were found to be 1.5 and 1.6 µM, and at 272 nm with a lower sensitivity were 6.2 and 6.3 µM, respectively. These results revealed the high potential of L-Cys/CdTe QDs as a chiroptical probe for chiral identification and detection of Tyr enantiomers using circular dichroism spectroscopy. It should be noted that pure L-Cys (1.5 × 10-4 M, as calculated using the total of free and surface attached concentrations of Cys in the probe solution) and L-Cys in the presence of Tyr enantiomers (50 µM) provided no CD signal. Hence, L-Cys could not be used for chiral identification and detection of Tyr enantiomers individually (Fig. S4).

In order to evaluated the selectivity of the proposed method, the CD response of L-Cys/QDs in the presence of the L-enantiomer of other amino acids were also investigated that revealed the high selectivity of this method for the recognition of Tyr enantiomers over other amino acids, Fig. 6 . We also tried to determine enantiomeric excess values of Tyr enantiomers using this method. In this case, sample solutions containing 50 µM of Tyr with different enantiomeric excess values were considered. As seen in Fig. 7 , the CD signal changes from a maximum positive value for the pure L-Tyr solution (100% enantiomeric excess of L-Tyr) to almost zero for the racemic mixture and to a minimum negative value for the pure D-Tyr solution (100% enantiomeric excess of D-Tyr). The recorded CD intensities of these solutions were linearly proportional to the enantiomeric excess values at both 232 and 272 nm with the LOD values of 1.2% and 4.6%, respectively.

In this work, the possibility of identification of the absolute chirality and quantitative analysis of Tyr enantiomers in the presence of L-Cys/CdTe QDs, as a chiroptical probe, over micromolar concentration range was provided by using both fluorescence and CD spectroscopies. The experimental results demonstrated that both fluorescence and CD spectroscopic methods could be utilized for the recognition of chiral Tyr enantiomers. However, the CD spectroscopy showed a higher potential for this purpose. While the L- and D-Tyr enantiomers exhibited no sensible CD spectra in the micromolar concentration ranges, they respectively displayed mirror positive and negative CD spectra in the presence of the L-Cys/CdTe QDs with intensities that were well correlated with the concentration of the Tyr enantiomers. It should be noted that while CD is one of the simple and reliable methods for the determination of the configuration and conformation of chiral compounds, this technique is not typically used as a quantitative analysis method, mainly due to its relatively high limit of detection. However, the use of chiral L-Cys/CdTe QDs as a suitable chiroptical probe can provide the possibility of using this technique for quantitative analysis by greatly reducing the detection limit.

Foroozan Feizi: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing- original draft. Mojtaba Shamsipur: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Ali Barati: Visualization, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Mohammad Bagher Gholivand: Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Farimah Mousavi: Methodology, Data curation, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support of this work by Iran Science Elites Federation and Research Council of Razi University is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105168.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
